title: US Navy Maintains Blockade on Iranian Crude Exports Amid Strait of Hormuz Standoff
summary: The US Navy continues enforcing a blockade on Iranian crude oil exports while the Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed, with a proposed Maritime Freedom Construct coalition awaiting conflict resolution.
contents:
## Maritime Standoff Continues in Persian Gulf

### Blockade Status
The US Navy maintains its blockade of Iranian crude oil exports as the Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed to commercial traffic. Iran has allowed only a trickle of vessels through the waterway since the February 28 outbreak of hostilities.

### Toll Demands
Iran has demanded payments from vessels seeking passage through the Strait, offering multiple payment options including fiat currency, digital assets, and even charitable donations. OFAC's new advisory explicitly warns against any such payments.

### Coalition Proposal
A State Department cable obtained by media outlets reveals an invitation to partner nations to join a new coalition called the "Maritime Freedom Construct" designed to enable ships to navigate the Strait. France, Britain, and others have held talks on contributing but conditioned participation on the conflict ending first.

### Energy Market Impact
Brent crude futures traded around $110 per barrel, reflecting the severe supply disruption. The Strait normally handles approximately 20% of global oil and gas shipments.

### UAE Response
UAE presidential adviser Anwar Gargash stated that "collective international will and provisions of international law" serve as the primary guarantors of freedom of navigation, adding that "no unilateral Iranian arrangements merits trust" following what he termed "treacherous aggression against all its neighbors."
